content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum


DATE: January 27, 1983


TO: File

FROM: Janis Shank McLean [signature]

RE: Items excluded under Section 82034
     M-83-018


The following items are not "investments" under Section
82034:

     1. A time or demand deposit in a financial institution;

     2. Shares in a credit union;

     3. Any insurance policy;

     4. An interest in a diversified mutual fund registered with
the SEC under the Investment Company Act of 1940:

          a. A mutual fund is also known as a management
company.

          b. A mutual fund is a company which owns a large
amount of diversified stocks. (Although diversified, the
stocks may all be of one type, such as stocks in various
types of natural resources; utilities; or money market
investments.)

               1) The investor buys a share in the mutual fund
and he or she does not own any interest in the stock or
investments that the mutual fund owns.

               2) The investor does not control how the mutual
fund handles its investments.

               3) The investor is aware of the specific
investments owned by the mutual fund.

               4) For a mutual fund to be "diversified", it must
have at least 75% of its total assets in: cash and
cash items (including receivables); government
securities; securities of other investment companies;
and securities of any one issuer to an amount no
greater than 5% of the value of the total assets of the
company and no more than 10% of the outstanding voting
securities of the issuer.

                                        - 1 -

5)    The exemption from reporting appears to be
based upon the fact that the filer has no investment
interest in specific stocks, but only in the mutual
fund, which has highly diversified interests and a very
small interest in any one company.

5.  A common trust fund created pursuant to Section 1564 of
the Financial Code:

     a.  Section 1564 allows any trust company to combine
the money from several trusts into one common trust fund.

     b.  This money is then invested.  The participating
fiduciary relationships do not have an ownership interest in
any of the particular investments held by the common trust
fund, but rather, each has an undivided interest in the fund
and its income.

     c.  See 7 Witkin, Summ. of Cal. Law, p.5425, Section
64.  See Rev. and Tax Code Section 17671.

     d.  Use of the common trust fund allows for
diversification (Witkin).

6.  Interests in defined benefit pension plans qualified
under Internal Revenue Code Section 401(a).  (See Section
82030(b)(11), M-82-173 and A-82-207.)

     a.  These pension plans are handled as trusts.

7.  Interests in profit sharing plans qualified under Int.
Rev. Code Section 401(a).  (See A-82-207.)

     a.  These profit sharing plans are handled as trusts.

JSM:km


- 2 -

sections.conclusion: The following items are not 'investments' under Section 82034: (1) accounts or demand deposits at financial institutions; (2) shares in a credit union; (3) any insurance policy; (4) interests in diversified mutual funds registered with the SEC under the Investment Company Act of 1940; (5) common trust funds created under Financial Code Section 1564; (6) interests in defined benefit pension plans qualified under Internal Revenue Code Section 401(a); and (7) interests in profit sharing plans qualified under Internal Revenue Code Section 401(a).
sections.question_synthetic: What items are excluded from the definition of 'investments' under Political Reform Act Section 82034?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC identifies seven categories of assets that are excluded from the definition of 'investments' under Section 82034, including mutual funds, common trust funds, and qualified pension/profit-sharing plans. The exclusion is based on the filer's lack of direct ownership interest in specific underlying securities and limited control over investment decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a memorandum-style advice letter with no formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION section headers. The document number is M-83-018. Some text is obscured or partially illegible due to document quality, but the substantive content regarding exclusions is clear. Author is Janis Shank McLean.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What items are excluded from the definition of 'investments' under Political Reform Act Section 82034?

CONCLUSION: The following items are not 'investments' under Section 82034: (1) accounts or demand deposits at financial institutions; (2) shares in a credit union; (3) any insurance policy; (4) interests in diversified mutual funds registered with the SEC under the Investment Company Act of 1940; (5) common trust funds created under Financial Code Section 1564; (6) interests in defined benefit pension plans qualified under Internal Revenue Code Section 401(a); and (7) interests in profit sharing plans qualified under Internal Revenue Code Section 401(a).

A time or demand deposit in a financial institution; 2. Shares in a credit union; 3. Any insurance policy; 4. An interest in a diversified mutual fund registered with the SEC under the Investment Company Act of 1940: a. A mutual fund is also known as a management company. b. A mutual fund is a company which owns a large amount of diversified stocks. (Although diversified, the stocks may all be of one type, such as stocks in various types of natural resources; utilities; or money market investments.) 1) The investor buys a share in the mutual fund and he or she does not own any interest in the stock or investments that the mutual fund owns. 2) The investor does not control how the mutual fund handles its investments. 3) The investor is aware of the specific investments owned by the mutual fund. 4) For a mutual fund to be "diversified", it must have at least 75% of its total assets in: cash and cash items (including receivables); government securities; securities of other investment companies; and securities of any one issuer to an amount no greater than 5% of the value of the total assets of the company and no more than 10% of the outstanding voting securities of the issuer. - 1 - 5) The exemption from reporting appears to be based upon the fact that the filer has no investment interest in specific stocks, but only in the mutual fund, which has highly diversified interests and a very small interest in any one company. 5. A common trust fund created pursuant to Section 1564 of the Financial Code: a. Section 1564 allows any trust company to combine the money from several trusts into one common trust fund. b. This money is then invested. The participating fiduciary relationships do not have an ownership interest in any of the particular investments held by the common trust fund, but rather, each has an undivided interest in the fund and its income. c. See 7 Witkin, Summ. of Cal. Law, p.5425, Section 64. See Rev. and Tax Code Section 17671. d. Use of the common trust fund allows for diversification (Witkin). 6. Interests in defined benefit pension plans qualified under Internal Revenue Code Section 401(a). (See Section 82030(b)(11), M-82-173 and A-82-207.) a. These pension plans are handled as trusts. 7. Interests in profit sharing plans qualified under Int. Rev. Code Section 401(a). (See A-82-207.) a. These profit sharing plans are handled as trusts. JSM:km - 2 -
embedding.summary: This FPPC memorandum clarifies that seven categories of assets—including deposit accounts, credit union shares, insurance policies, diversified mutual funds, common trust funds, and qualified pension and profit-sharing plans—are excluded from the definition of 'investments' under Political Reform Act Section 82034.
